title: IF
summary: The IF function returns one value when a condition is TRUE and another when it is FALSE. It is the foundation of decision-making logic in Excel.
jobUseCases: Flagging sales reps as 'Target Met' or 'Below Target' based on monthly revenue
Calculating bonus eligibility for employees who exceed a performance threshold
Categorizing invoices as 'Overdue' or 'On Time' by comparing due dates to today
Assigning pass/fail grades to candidates in a recruitment assessment tracker
Applying a discount only when an order value crosses a minimum amount

examples.title: Simple pass/fail check
examples.explanation: If Amit's score in B2 is 40 or higher, the cell shows 'Pass'; otherwise it shows 'Fail'. Text values must be wrapped in double quotes.

examples.title: Target achievement flag
examples.explanation: Compares Priya's monthly sales in C2 against a 100,000 target. Returns 'Target Met' when sales reach or exceed the goal.

examples.title: Nested IF for grade bands
examples.explanation: Checks Rahul's score against multiple thresholds in order. The first condition that is TRUE wins, so ranges are evaluated from highest to lowest.

examples.title: Returning a calculation
examples.explanation: If Sara's order value in E2 exceeds 50,000, it returns a 10% incentive; otherwise it returns 0. value_if_true and value_if_false can be formulas, not just text.

commonMistakes: Forgetting double quotes around text results, e.g. writing Pass instead of "Pass", which causes a #NAME? error
Putting quotes around numbers like "100" so they are treated as text and break later calculations
Mismatched or missing parentheses when nesting multiple IF statements
Ordering nested IF conditions incorrectly (e.g. checking >=60 before >=90), which makes higher bands unreachable
Using a comma vs semicolon as the argument separator depending on regional settings
practiceTips: Build complex logic step by step: get a single IF working before nesting more
For more than 3-4 conditions, switch to IFS or a lookup table to keep formulas readable
Use cell references for thresholds instead of hardcoding numbers so they are easy to update
